dateStatements.label: Composition
dateStatements.notes: The lower bound is Gruffudd's initiating letter; the upper bound is Grey's dated report that he had sent the answer and enclosed a copy.
dateStatements.value: after 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400

entities.description: A Middle English political letter written after 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400. Grey recapitulates Gruffudd's complaints, denies plans to attack loyal subjects, invokes royal law, and threatens hanging in a closing passage containing seven lines of rhyming doggerel.
entities.label: Reginald Grey's reply to Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd

rights.basis: The letter was created in 1400 and published in a nineteenth-century edition; any U.S. copyright term has expired.
rights.component: Underlying medieval letter text

bibliographic.citation: Francis Charles Hingeston, ed., Royal and Historical Letters during the Reign of Henry the Fourth, King of England and of France, and Lord of Ireland, vol. I, A.D. 1399-1404, Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores 18 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860), xxii-xxiii, 35-38.
bibliographic.title: Royal and Historical Letters during the Reign of Henry the Fourth, King of England and of France, and Lord of Ireland

entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00124. Letter XVI prints Grey's reply on pp.35-38 from Cotton Cleopatra F III, f.70b, with extensive notes reconstructing the three-letter sequence and selected passages reprinted from Ellis.
entities.label: Francis Charles Hingeston, Royal and Historical Letters, volume I (1860)
notes: The preface says the volume collects letters of historical interest not already published in the principal earlier collections and draws from Cotton, Harley, Public Record Office, Bodleian, and episcopal sources.
Hingeston's headnote reads: 'MS. Cotton. Cleop. F. III. fol. 70 b.-On paper; a contemporaneous MS.'

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1860, before 1930, and is in the U.S. public domain.
rights.component: 1860 edition text and editorial matter

dateStatements.label: Codex materials
dateStatements.value: last quarter of the fourteenth century to first quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A 213-folio collection of Privy Council and royal-administrative materials, much concerning the Welsh rebellion and the 1415 French expedition. The current whole-record title broadly calls the materials 'all originals,' but Hingeston's component-level evidence identifies the target at f.70b as a contemporary answer-copy; that component identification controls here. Grey's 23 June report is at f.70 and Gruffudd's 11 June letter at f.72.

holding.notes: All manuscripts and archives are consulted at the British Library in London; current physical requests require a valid Reader Pass and the exact shelfmark.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Cleopatra F III

physicalDescription.extent: 213 folios: 2 early modern endleaves plus 211 further folios; target copy at f.70b, related report at f.70, and initiating letter at f.72
notes: Leaves were damaged by fire in 1731.
The present binding is British Museum, 1961.
Hingeston's paper-support statement describes the attached answer-copy; the current catalog's parchment statement describes its host f.70.
physicalDescription.support: Mixed parchment and paper; the target is a paper document attached to the verso of parchment host f.70, while f.72 lies in a paper run

next_steps: Inspect or obtain complete color photography of f.70 recto and verso, the attached paper, and adjacent surfaces.
Do not substitute Ruthin, the dateline of the separate enclosing report, for the reply's unstated composition place.
openQuestions.question: Where exactly was Grey's reply composed, and does the f.70b paper preserve an address, dateline, endorsement, or omitted text that would answer this?

next_steps: Ask British Library staff for condition and cataloging detail before ordering.
Request overall recto/verso and attachment-detail photography if conservation policy permits.
openQuestions.question: What are the precise dimensions, attachment method, orientation, and current foliation of the paper answer-copy mounted on parchment host f.70?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services about internal, legacy, microfilm, or unpublished photography.
If none exists, seek a quote for whole-object color photography and preserve the exact f.70b/f.70/f.72 work mapping.

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00124.xml; the U-M encoding, abstract historical letter, Hingeston and Ellis editions, manuscript, scans, catalog description, and Davis article are separate entities.
The cataloged historical work is Grey's reply, not the whole three-letter dossier. Hingeston's two groups of Gruffudd excerpts and one Grey-to-Prince excerpt are supporting content parts.

Hingeston calls the answer-copy a contemporary paper manuscript pasted on f.70b, while the current British Library material inventory calls f.70 parchment. The attached document's carrier and the host leaf are modeled as separate physical layers, so the statements are not treated as contradictory.
Hingeston's main text is directly related to f.70b. His supporting excerpts are passage-level reprints from Ellis, who directly transcribed f.70 and f.72.

The current British Library catalog's Digitised Content field is blank and no viewer link was found as of 2026-07-13. This dated negative finding does not prove that no internal, unpublished, or legacy photography exists.
The pinned CME root LICENSE uses Unlicense language framed as software. Its application to corpus XML/data/markup remains unresolved and is not broadened into a high-confidence rights claim.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00124, which encodes Francis Charles Hingeston's Letter XVI on printed pp.35-38 of his 1860 Royal and Historical Letters, volume I. Hingeston directly transcribed Grey's contemporary paper answer-copy pasted at British Library Cotton MS Cleopatra F III, f.70b. For supporting passages from Gruffudd's 11 June letter at f.72 and Grey's 23 June report at f.70, however, Hingeston explicitly reprinted Henry Ellis's 1827 transcriptions, so those paths run through Ellis rather than directly to the codex. Complete copies of both nineteenth-century volumes and Norman Davis's supporting 1967 article are cached with fixity. No public manuscript facsimile was found in the current British Library record; physical consultation and paid whole-object photography remain request routes.

catalogSummary.author: Reginald Grey, Lord Grey de Ruthyn
catalogSummary.date: After 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400
catalogSummary.editor: Francis Charles Hingeston; supporting excerpts transcribed by Henry Ellis
catalogSummary.region: The Welsh Marches, especially the Ruthin lordship, Chirkland, and Oswestry; exact place of composition unstated
catalogSummary.title: Lord Grey de Ruthyn to Griffith ap David ap Griffith

catalogingSubject.label: Lord Grey de Ruthyn to Griffith ap David ap Griffith
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Reginald Grey's Middle English reply to Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd, written after Gruffudd's letter of 11 June and no later than Grey's report of 23 June 1400. It is not the whole three-letter dossier, Cotton MS Cleopatra F III as a physical manuscript, Hingeston's volume or editorial notes as a whole, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. Hingeston's paratext and his excerpts from the two related letters remain separately scoped content parts.

contentParts.label: Grey's reply to Gruffudd

locators.value: DIV1 direct child P elements (8), excluding embedded NOTE content
printed pp.35-38; Internet Archive PDF pages 155-158
f.70b, attached paper copy on verso of a parchment host leaf

contentParts.summary: The complete represented reply: address, point-by-point recapitulation, rebuttal, accusation concerning two stolen horses, royal-law argument, and closing threat of hanging.

contentParts.label: Hingeston's heading and editorial paratext

locators.value: pp.35-38, plus contextual preface discussion at pp.xxii-xxiii

notes: Some NOTE containers also embed the separately modeled historical-letter excerpts; item counts therefore document XML/editorial containers, not mutually exclusive prose units.
The printed closing 'Written, etc.' is Hingeston's editorial abbreviation and is not cataloged as a historical explicit or title.
contentParts.summary: The item heading, source and support statement, marginal synopsis, identifications, textual note, quotation framing, and reconstruction of the three-letter sequence.

contentParts.label: Excerpts from Gruffudd's letter of 11 June 1400

locators.value: quoted text within NOTE[3] and NOTE[5]; 9 quoted P units plus editorial framing
Letter II, printed pp.5-7; Commons PDF pages 24-26
f.72; exact side and extent not established

notes: This is not a complete reprint of Ellis's Letter II: Hingeston omits the salutation and at least one sentence between his two quoted runs.
contentParts.summary: Selected passages from Gruffudd's initiating complaint, quoted in two footnotes so readers can compare Grey's recapitulation with the complained-of betrayal, the stolen horses, mutual threats, and the dated subscription.

contentParts.label: Excerpt from Grey's report to the Prince of Wales

locators.value: Letter I, printed pp.1-4, especially p.4; Commons PDF pages 20-23
f.70; the answer-copy is pasted on its reverse

contentParts.summary: The sentence in Grey's 23 June 1400 report stating that he sent Gruffudd's letter to the Prince together with a copy of the answer he had sent back.

dateStatements.display: After 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400
notes: Gruffudd's initiating letter is dated 11 June. Grey's 23 June report says that he had sent an answer and enclosed its copy; Hingeston therefore dates the answer on or before 23 June. The exact day and place written are not stated in the represented text.

dateStatements.display: Contemporary copy extant with Grey's report of 23 June 1400

extent.description: Four modeled content parts contain 8 direct main-letter paragraphs, 1 heading plus 7 note containers of editorial paratext, 9 quoted paragraphs from Gruffudd's letter, and 1 quoted passage from Grey's report to the Prince, for 26 navigational items. These units overlap at the representation level where a NOTE container frames quoted text, so they are not a count of independent historical works. The XML has 1 DIV1, 1 HEAD, 23 P (8 direct and 15 inside notes), 7 NOTE, 5 PB, 3 HI, and 4 SUP elements. Davis identifies the closing threat as seven lines of rhyming doggerel even though the XML serializes it inside one P rather than L elements.

formStatements.description: A prose letter whose closing threat forms an intentional seven-line rhyming doggerel passage.
Most of Grey's reply is epistolary prose.

scope.description: The address, recapitulation, rebuttal, and most of the threat

formStatements.description: Norman Davis identifies Grey's seven lines as an intentional example of occasional rhyming doggerel.

scope.description: Seven-line rhyming threat beginning 'But we hoope we shalle do the a pryve thyng'

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English editorial headings and notes are paratext, not a second language facet for the abstract medieval work.

scope.description: The historical letter and both embedded historical-letter excerpts

notes: TEXT/@LANG='enm' describes the historical text layers. Modern editorial English in Hingeston's heading and notes is deliberately excluded from work-level viewer facets.
CME00124's seven-line doggerel is serialized inside a prose P; the mixed-form classification rests on Davis's explicit scholarly identification, not on XML L tagging.
Hingeston's quoted supporting passages are mediated through Ellis. Their common manuscript shelfmark does not justify bypassing Ellis in the modeled passage lineage.
The XML proofing date 8-31-16 is repository encoding history, not a work, witness, edition, or publication date.

next_steps: Inspect the complete f.70b attached paper document and any endorsement, address, or lost-text evidence.
Do not infer Ruthin merely from the dateline of the separate 23 June enclosing report.
openQuestions.question: Where exactly was Grey's reply composed?

next_steps: Request color images of all of f.70 recto and verso, transmitted-light or attachment detail if the British Library permits, and adjacent surfaces.
Keep the paper document and parchment host leaf as distinct physical layers until directly inspected.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact physical construction, attachment method, dimensions, and present foliation of the paper answer-copy mounted at f.70b?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether unpublished or legacy photography exists.
If ordering, request the whole physical object and map the target separately to f.70b, with related letters at f.70 and f.72.
openQuestions.question: Is there a complete public digital facsimile of Cotton MS Cleopatra F III outside the current British Library catalog interface?

next_steps: Request a path-specific clarification from the repository maintainer.
Until clarified, do not broaden the root software wording into a confirmed public-domain grant for markup or data.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally apply to XML markup and data in CME00124?

notes: This is historical setting and association, not a claim that the letter was composed at a particular Marcher place.
Grey was Lord Grey of Ruthin and his 23 June enclosing report was written at Ruthin; neither fact proves that the reply itself was composed there.

responsibilities.agentName: Reginald Grey, Lord Grey de Ruthyn
notes: Authorship concerns the historical letter; the surviving f.70b item is described by Hingeston as a contemporary copy attached to Grey's report to the Prince of Wales, not as an autograph.

responsibilities.agentName: Reginald Grey, Lord Grey de Ruthyn

scope.description: The complete printed item and its represented editorial layers
Passages that Hingeston explicitly reprints from Ellis's 1827 edition

subjects.label: Lordship conflict in the Welsh Marches

assertion.notes: Historical context supplied by Hingeston and the current manuscript catalog; Owain Glyndŵr is not named in Grey's reply.

subjects.label: Prelude and context to the Glyndŵr rising

summaries.value: Reginald Grey answers Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd's complaints point by point, rejects responsibility for John Wele's alleged betrayal, rebuts threats against Grey's men and lordship, invokes loyalty to the king and his law, and threatens Gruffudd with hanging for receiving stolen horses. The closing threat shifts from prose into seven lines of intentional rhyming doggerel.

CME00124 encodes Hingeston's Letter XVI on printed pp.35-38, including the main reply, its heading, marginal summary, witness note, textual notes, extensive excerpts from Gruffudd's 11 June letter reprinted from Ellis, and the final editorial reconstruction with an excerpt from Grey's 23 June report to the Prince of Wales. The initiating and enclosing letters are supporting related works, not parts of Grey's reply.

Hingeston describes the target as a contemporary paper manuscript pasted on the back of Grey's letter to the Prince at f.70b. The current British Library codex inventory describes f.70 itself as parchment. These claims are compatible if the paper letter-copy is an attached document mounted on the verso of a parchment host leaf; carrier and host support must not be conflated.

notes: This is an editorial heading rather than a manuscript title or authorial superscription.

Gruffuth ap David ap Gruffuth. We send the greting welle, but no thyng with goode hert.
